name: Moksha
shortDescription: Moksha is the liberation from the cycle of death and rebirth (samsara) in Hinduism, representing the ultimate spiritual goal.
description: Moksha, also known as liberation, emancipation, or release, is a central concept in Hinduism representing the ultimate goal of human existence. It signifies freedom from the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth (samsara) and the associated suffering. Achieving moksha involves transcending the limitations of the ego, desires, and attachments that bind individuals to the material world. Different schools of Hindu philosophy offer various paths to moksha, including Jnana Yoga (the path of knowledge), Bhakti Yoga (the path of devotion), Karma Yoga (the path of selfless action), and Raja Yoga (the path of meditation). Each path emphasizes different aspects of spiritual practice, but all aim to realize the true nature of the self (Atman) and its unity with the ultimate reality (Brahman). The concept is closely tied to dharma (righteous conduct), artha (material prosperity), and kama (desire), which are the other three purusharthas (aims of human life). While dharma, artha, and kama are important for a fulfilling life, moksha is considered the highest aspiration, representing the cessation of all suffering and the attainment of eternal bliss. The state of moksha is often described as a state of profound peace, knowledge, and bliss, where the individual self merges with the divine. Achieving moksha requires dedicated spiritual practice, ethical conduct, and the grace of a guru or divine being.
